title: Rush mat, round plait
subtitle: Small round mat or coaster from plaited soft rush
excerpt: A small round mat or coaster in plaited soft rush, built up from the centre as a flat spiral. Rush is a different material to willow: softer, more pliable, and worked flat. A first project for rush craft.
sourceNotes: Edlin, H.L., Woodland Crafts in Britain (1949) on rush work. Caulfield and Saward, Dictionary of Needlework (1882) on plaited rush mats.

glossaryTerms.definition: Juncus effusus, the common UK rush. Harvested in midsummer when the stem is firm, dried to a buff-olive colour for plaiting.
A basketry construction method where a continuous element is coiled in a flat spiral, each row lashed or plaited to the previous one.
A two-element weave where two strands alternate, one crossing over the other between each upright.

content.text: Soft rush
produces flat mats and coasters by
coiling
: a bundle of rushes is coiled from the centre outward while a binding rush holds each row to the last. The
pairing
technique locks each new coil to the previous one. Rush is much softer than willow and can be worked on the lap without a shaving horse or bodkin for most of the construction.
Secateurs are sharp. Close them when setting down; keep fingers clear of the cutting path.
Dry willow ends are stiff and springy when trimmed. Guard your face when snipping tall stakes.
Soaking water: do not leave willow soaking for more than 7 days. Stagnant water grows mould on the rods.
Workspace: low stable seating, knees together as a lap-board for the work-in-progress.
Materials

Dried soft rush: a bundle 8 cm in circumference and 90 cm long (about 30 to 40 individual rush stems). Soak in cold water for 5 minutes before use; soft rush needs only a short soak, not the long soaks needed for willow rods. Wrap in a damp cloth and leave for 20 minutes to mellow before starting.
Tools
Secateurs for trimming ends; a bodkin for opening paths in the weave at the centre; no rapping iron needed.
Method

Take four rush stems and fold them at their centre. You now have eight stem ends forming the starting bundle. Wrap a binding rush around this bundle twice at the centre fold, then fold the whole bundle in half again: the result is a small 16-stem core.

Hold the core in the left hand and begin coiling: bring the binding rush around the outside of the core, then back through the centre, working in a clockwise direction. With each pass, the core extends outward as new rush stems are added to it. Keep the coil flat on the knee or a board.
Use
pairing

to lock each new coil row to the previous: pass the binding rush in front of one section of the last row and behind the next, then cross it with a second binding rush doing the same in the opposite direction. This keeps each row from pulling away from the last.
Continue adding rush to the working bundle and coiling outward until the mat reaches 18 cm in diameter.
To finish the outer edge: tuck each rush stem end back through the last two rows of the mat with the bodkin. Pull the tuck tight and trim the surplus with secateurs.
Finishing
Leave the mat flat to dry for 24 hours under a book or flat weight to prevent warping. Rush shrinks and the mat will firm up as it dries.
Variations
Square mat: coil in straight sections rather than curves, turning 90-degree corners on each row. More difficult to keep flat.
Two-colour mat: alternate bleached rush with natural brown for a striped pattern.
Larger placemat: extend to 28 cm diameter. The centre coiling technique is identical; the outer rows simply increase the circuit count.

items.symptom: Mat curves upward at the edges as it dries
items.cause: Centre section too tight; outer rows too loose
items.fix: Keep consistent tension throughout. Press the mat flat under a weighted board within the first hour of drying, before it sets.

items.cause: Rush stem too dry or a weak section at a node
items.fix: Join in a new binding rush at a rush stem end. Overlap the old and new rush by 4 cm; the overlapping section holds by friction.

items.symptom: Rush ends work loose at the outer edge
items.cause: Tuck too short or not pulled tight enough
items.fix: Each tuck must pass through at least two rows of the mat. Use the bodkin to open a path that crosses two rows, then pull the tuck firm before trimming.
